speaker: interviewee
text: That's my wife, Luba Landau. And the underground work which she-- the Polish passport was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I know that you both survived the war years in Warsaw, which in itself is quite remarkable. Luba, I turn to you-- I also-- David, told us that you were married in '41, and you've known one another since children. So in the miracle, that two Jewish people were able to survive in Warsaw, what, how, what instinct provided-- provoked that miracle? Do you have an answer to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the most difficult thing to sum up. Because a lot of it depended on luck, on sheer luck, I think, and also on some situations of which we were only partly aware. Because we helped, in a way, in rescuing ourselves, in a-- in a-- in a method that we didn't know ourselves that will be all right. The only thing that really was the most important for me, and I think for David, was that we were very keen, if you can use this word, to survive. Not so much to survive, but to see Hitlers and the Germans going to hell. That we were the ones to see it, and it happened that we had, in a way, our very sad victory, I would say. Because we lost everybody around us. But we saw this dreadful killing machine go. And this was one of the most fantastic things that ever happened to a human being. And-- and this idea, and this situation, and will, kept us in part, alive, and made us do things that normally a human being wouldn't want to do. Because at the end of the war, I didn't want to live. I don't know whether David told you about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've had it. And therefore, now that we are alive, and that we managed to live, more or less, a normal life. Which it didn't, not in the beginning, want to admit the whole stories that we went through. Because we just wanted to be normal people. But when it all started to come out, we decided to tell our story, which I think it's most unbelievable to the majority of people. Because it's very, very difficult to imagine something like that. And that's about all I want to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. David and Luba, 56 years of life together, and I know you knew--

speaker: interviewer
text: 54 Marriage, but the war started '39, so that's 56 years of miracles. And I know you know one another since children. Do you have a message for one another?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think you can give a person a recipe for life. I can only give one recipe-- love. I think love helps a lot. And love is all you can give a person.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the children and to the rest of the Jews, which are--

speaker: interviewee
text: And to our children, our friends, and I would say to our little country, in which we don't even live, but tremendous love.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I feel-- is to forget about hatred, to forget about hating-- the word hate. One thing which I will say to the young ones-- beware.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, always have your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very loud word-- beware. Don't hate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, David Landau, thank you very much. Luba, I put you on the spot, but thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: David, please tell us about this photo. That's my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the place. I have been there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you, David. David, now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother of my father. I don't know the names. And that's his father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. David, please identify this photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the youngest, [? Yosef. ?] That's me, David.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Maury, ?] Metek, And this is Eliazur.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Thank you very much. Rolling. David, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's me in 1939, the beginning of the year. And next to it, the small one is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is the girl, which I took it in the ghetto, which I told you about.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Naomi Titkin. That's her father and mother in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, David. Please explain who this person is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my cousin, which was--

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war in France-- Maurice. [? Borenstein ?], a [INAUDIBLE] fighter, and one of the first hundred de Gaulle's soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived the war. He's paralyzed.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this photo is myself, dressed as a German officer during the Jewish resistance in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: '43. Thank you, David. Who's in that photo, please, David?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's here, myself in the 1973 war in Israel, as a volunteer in the Israeli army. You want the papers too for it? I got the papers from the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife's sister, the older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Who's in this photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is of my daughter, Miriam Alexandra.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who's in-- who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Miriam Alexandra, climbing a tree in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please, David, in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Miriam at the wedding day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. David, who's in this photo, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the young man standing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is myself and my wife, Luba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. David, who's in this photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael is my grandson with my daughter.
